project.name: BADGR Harness
project.scope: Entire conversation handoff through Phase 4 preflight and validated orchestrator JSON runs

high_level_goal.summary: Build a cost-efficient BADGR harness that favors deterministic routing, schema validation, local-first execution, and tightly controlled escalation instead of loose multi-agent sprawl.
core_intent: The system should behave like a governed harness, not an always-on swarm of overlapping agents.
Cheaper capable paths should be used first; premium reasoning should be reserved for hard planning, debugging, synthesis, or escalation.
The build should integrate around real BADGR operating projects rather than reinventing everything from scratch.

project_evolution.start_state: The early conversation began in design-phase mode around a cost-tiered execution graph and reviewable architecture, not a finished implementation.
project_evolution.middle_state: The direction evolved into a practical BADGR-wide harness strategy after grounding the work in surrounding BADGR systems such as lead generation, web performance, market-intel, content/social, and agent experiments.
project_evolution.current_state: The conversation has now progressed into active Phase 4 validation, with preflight checks, model-lane alignment, and structured orchestrator output testing becoming the immediate operational focus.

must_preserve: Local first where quality is acceptable.
Schema-validated structured outputs before trusting downstream behavior.
Explicit retry, fallback, and escalation logic.
Minimal moving parts until complexity is proven necessary.
Integration around existing BADGR assets instead of ornamental rebuilds.
anti_patterns: Agent sprawl and duplicated intelligence roles.
Premature heavy orchestration with unclear ROI.
Blind trust in local-model structured output without validation.
Optional heavy subsystems becoming core requirements without a concrete use case.

badgr_context.why_this_matters: The harness is intended to unify or govern real BADGR work rather than stay as an abstract framework.

phase_map.name: Vision and constraints
phase_map.focus: Define BADGR harness purpose, operating boundaries, and anti-sprawl philosophy.

phase_map.name: Architecture framing
phase_map.focus: Separate interface, governance, deterministic tools, local models, retrieval, and premium reasoning.

phase_map.name: Grounding in real BADGR assets
phase_map.focus: Tie the harness to actual BADGR projects and working department foundations.

phase_map.name: Live validation
phase_map.focus: Preflight, model alignment, routing-lane confirmation, orchestrator output validation, and observability verification.

phase_map.name: Schema hardening and observability
phase_map.focus: Tighten planning/general schemas, inspect logs/reports, and verify fallback/escalation traces.

phase_map.name: Controlled expansion
phase_map.focus: Add more realistic BADGR workflows after core trust boundaries are stable.

phase_1_summary.summary: Phase 1 established the harness as a governed, cost-minimized execution system with strict routing, strict schemas, and a strong bias toward deterministic or local-first operation.
deliverables: Continuation handoff expectations defined for Claude Code.

phase_2_summary.summary: Phase 2 reframed the work from pure concept into a practical harness package and continuation path, with emphasis on inspecting and validating the new package before assuming integration success.
deliverables: Cold-start review assumptions documented for Claude Code.

phase_4_current_read.summary: The current codebase direction supports a real local harness with preflight checks, model registry routing, validation, retry/fallback/escalation behavior, and JSON-only output handling.
what_is_working_directionally: Model roles are explicitly separated in models.yaml.
The orchestrator normalizes goals, chooses models by task lane, validates outputs, retries once, falls back, and can escalate to supervisor.
The validator enforces stricter requirements for classification and code/extraction style outputs than for generic tasks.
remaining_gaps: Planning/general responses currently use the base response shape instead of a dedicated planning schema.
Logs and reports should be inspected as part of the next validation pass.
Git is not initialized in the project directory, so change tracking is currently outside Git workflows.

why_this_logic_exists: Routing is explicit because hidden agent behavior increases cost and debugging complexity.
Validation exists because model outputs can be plausible but structurally wrong.
Retry and fallback exist because a local-first stack needs graceful recovery before escalation.
Supervisor escalation exists because some requests are ambiguous, underspecified, or too complex for the worker path.
specific_logic_patterns_to_understand: Primary model selection is task-lane based, not arbitrary.
If the first worker output fails validation, the orchestrator retries once with a stronger formatting instruction.
If the retry fails, the orchestrator uses the configured fallback lane.
If fallback fails, the supervisor can either return a valid result or request clarification.

assumptions: This is no longer only conceptual; the harness has entered real validation behavior.
The governing philosophy still matters more than adding more layers or tools.
Any next edits should preserve deterministic-first and validation-first principles.
The next meaningful improvement is hardening schemas, validating logs/reports, and testing failure paths more deliberately.
do_not_assume: Do not assume planning output is schema-complete just because it is valid JSON.
Do not assume logs and reports are correct until inspected.
Do not assume Git-backed diffing/history exists yet.
Do not assume every lane has the same level of validator strictness.

next_actions: Inspect generated logs and reports from orchestrator runs.
Add a dedicated planning schema so steps and related fields are enforced.
Run deliberate clarification and fallback tests under saved-file validation.
Optionally initialize Git in the project folder before broader refactors.
Only after those checks, expand toward more realistic BADGR workflows.
